comment_text.sn7.22:1.2: Khomadussa (“linen-cloth”) was probably named for the main product of the town. Flax for linen prefers temperate climes and is grown in northern India.
comment_text.sn7.22:1.4: Brāhmaṇagahapatikā means “brahmins and householders” rather than “brahmin householders”. It is a term for the “landed gentry”. A householder is a landowner, not just a lay person. Council discussions were for the upper class, not the general populace (negamajānapadā).
comment_text.sn7.22:1.8: “Council rules” (sabhādhamma) appears only here. The Buddha plays with it, punning sabhā (“council”) with sabbhi (“the virtuous”), and dhamma (“rule”) with “principle, morality”. The text does not make clear what their problem was with the Buddha’s approach. If, as is usually understood, the rules of such councils were similar to the rules of the monastic code, the Council would be constituted of residents within the area, and once business was underway, it should not be interrupted.

root_text.sn7.22:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sakkesu viharati khomadussaṁ nāmaṁ sakyānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn7.22:1.3: Atha kho bhagavā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya khomadussaṁ nigamaṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn7.22:1.4: Tena kho pana samayena khomadussakā brāhmaṇagahapatikā sabhāyaṁ sannipatitā honti kenacideva karaṇīyena, devo ca ekamekaṁ phusāyati.
root_text.sn7.22:1.5: Atha kho bhagavā yena sā sabhā tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn7.22:1.6: Addasaṁsu khomadussakā brāhmaṇagahapatikā bhagavantaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
root_text.sn7.22:1.8: “ke ca muṇḍakā samaṇakā, ke ca sabhādhammaṁ jānissantī”ti?
root_text.sn7.22:1.9: Atha kho bhagavā khomadussake brāhmaṇagahapatike gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn7.22:2.2: Santo na te ye na vadanti dhammaṁ;
root_text.sn7.22:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, khomadussakā brāhmaṇagahapatikā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn7.22:3.3: Seyyathāpi, bho gotama, nikkujjitaṁ vā ukkujjeyya, paṭicchannaṁ vā vivareyya, mūḷhassa vā maggaṁ ācikkheyya, andhakāre vā telapajjotaṁ dhāreyya: ‘cakkhumanto rūpāni dakkhantī’ti; evamevaṁ bhotā gotamena anekapariyāyena dhammo pakāsito.
root_text.sn7.22:3.4: Ete mayaṁ bhavantaṁ gotamaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāma dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn7.22:3.5: Upāsake no bhavaṁ gotamo dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupete saraṇaṁ gate”ti.

translation_text.sn7.22:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, where they have a town named Khomadussa.
translation_text.sn7.22:1.3: Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Khomadussa for alms.
translation_text.sn7.22:1.4: Now at that time the brahmins and householders of Khomadussa were gathered in the council hall for some business, while a gentle rain drizzled down.
translation_text.sn7.22:1.5: Then the Buddha approached that council.
translation_text.sn7.22:1.6: The brahmins and householders saw the Buddha coming off in the distance,
translation_text.sn7.22:1.8: “Who are these shavelings, these fake ascetics? Don’t they understand the council rules?”
translation_text.sn7.22:1.9: Then the Buddha addressed the brahmins and householders of Khomadussa in verse:
translation_text.sn7.22:2.1: “If good people are not present it is no true council;
translation_text.sn7.22:2.2: and those whose speak against principle <j>are not good people.
translation_text.sn7.22:2.3: Having given up greed, hate, and delusion,
translation_text.sn7.22:2.4: speakers of principle are good people.”
translation_text.sn7.22:3.1: When he had spoken, the brahmins and householders of Khomadussa said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.22:3.3: As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways.
translation_text.sn7.22:3.4: We go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn7.22:3.5: From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge for life.”
translation_text.sn7.22:4.7: The Linked Discourses with Brahmins are complete.
